evidence_quotes.quote: "ad free" unlimited subscriptions only for adds to be suddenly added to subscribed accounts, that could get removed with a "higher tier subscription" (ex: prime video, youtube, netflix etc.), which essentially forces the consumer to pay higher and higher subscription rates to avoid advertisements.
The predatory structure of microtransactions and gacha games (Notable examples: Dragon Raja, Genshin Impact, EA microtransactions).
The fact that game publishers can remove access from legally purchased videogames, with no refund or end of life option.

companies not disclosing to the consumers if they're talking to a chatbot when contacting support through their website and not giving the consumer an option to talk to a customer support employee that is not "send an e-mail".

influencers being able to "feature" a scam or a business with suspicious and possibly illegal business practices, and deny accountability by claiming their content was a "joke", without disclosing as much anywhere in the video or post, nor placing disclaimers to "not attempt to copy them".
influencers promoting gambling behavior especially those with really young audiences, by either actually gambling online, or playing predatory games.

paragraphs.text: I agree that a comprehensive legal framework for digital products is very much needed, and I hope that this act will be addressing things such as: - The predatory structure of microtransactions and gacha games (Notable examples: Dragon Raja, Genshin Impact, EA microtransactions). - the fact that consumers were promised "ad free" unlimited subscriptions only for adds to be suddenly added to subscribed accounts, that could get removed with a "higher tier subscription" (ex: prime video, youtube, netflix etc.), which essentially forces the consumer to pay higher and higher subscription rates to avoid advertisements. - The fact that game publishers can remove access from legally purchased videogames, with no refund or end of life option. At the same time the publishers are not willing to let go of the copyright, which makes it illegal for users to access the game they legally purchased, through different means. Either they provide refunds, or they lose their copyrights. - The fact that console makers can make consumers sign a EULA that allows them to remotely digitally ban access to a legally purchased physical console and their account making it useless to the consumer (Nintendo). - The alleged influence of payment processors to videogame distributors, where the processors can threaten to "not process payments" unless the distributor complies with their conditions. - influencers being able to "feature" a scam or a business with suspicious and possibly illegal business practices, and deny accountability by claiming their content was a "joke", without disclosing as much anywhere in the video or post, nor placing disclaimers to "not attempt to copy them". - influencers promoting gambling behavior especially those with really young audiences, by either actually gambling online, or playing predatory games. - influencers not disclosing advertisements. - companies not disclosing to the consumers if they're talking to a chatbot when contacting support through their website and not giving the consumer an option to talk to a customer support employee that is not "send an e-mail".
